title: UCB — gMG — Synthesis
html:
gMG Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-25 Studies analyzed: 7 ZoomRx deliverables (proposal, kick-off, 5 check-ins) Data basis: 40 HCP-Patient conversations (22 Phase 1 + 18 Phase 2), collected June 2025 through March 2026 Method: Cross-deliverable pattern matching against thesis tracker. NOTE: Data is from check-in decks, not final reports — patterns are directional, pending final analysis.

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: Vyvgart Owns the gMG Treatment Decision — UCB Brands Are Mentioned, Not Prescribed

This is the single most consistent finding across all available ZoomRx check-in data.

Proposal (March 2025): ZoomRx's own POV notes: "Among branded therapies, VYVGART is highly preferred by HCPs followed by ULTOMIRIS." Even before the study began, ZoomRx flagged Vyvgart's dominance.

Phase 1 Check-ins (Jul-Aug 2025): Early conversation data showed Vyvgart (IV+Hytrulo) accumulating mentions at 3-4x the rate of any UCB brand. By the August 14 check-in, Rystiggo had 3 mentions and 1 prescription; Zilbrysq had 1 mention and 0 prescriptions. Vyvgart already had 18 mentions.

Phase 2 Final (April 2026): Across all 40 conversations: Vyvgart had 31 mentions / 17 prescriptions. Rystiggo had 13 mentions / 3 prescriptions. Zilbrysq had 4 mentions / 0 prescriptions. Imaavy had 12 mentions / 3 prescriptions. Uplizna had 5 mentions / 3 prescriptions.

What this means together: Rystiggo is in the conversation but not winning the decision. Zilbrysq is barely in the conversation at all. The pattern is consistent from earliest data through final count — not a sampling artifact. HCPs know about UCB's products but do not default to them. This is a conversion problem, not an awareness problem.

Pattern 2: When HCPs Move Away From Vyvgart, They Go to Imaavy or Complement — Not to Rystiggo

Aug 5/6 Check-in: One conversation (Conv #03) featured a patient who had previously tried Vyvgart and experienced chest pain, leading to hospital visits. The HCP did NOT switch to Rystiggo (same FcRn mechanism class). Instead, the HCP positioned Imaavy (different FcRn agent with continuous dosing) and discussed keeping the patient on Soliris in the interim.

Aug 14 Check-in: One KOL (neuromuscular, T1) directly compared Rystiggo to Vyvgart and positioned it as "pretty similar to the way the Vyvgart works" with a "little worse" dosing cycle (6 weeks on + 4 weeks off vs. Vyvgart's 4 weeks). This "similar but worse" framing is the opposite of what UCB wants.

April 2026 data: Imaavy accumulated 3 prescriptions in Phase 2 alone (despite launching in April 2025, mid-study). Meanwhile, Rystiggo added only 1 more prescription in Phase 2. Uplizna, which launched in December 2025, had 3 prescriptions in Phase 2 — matching Imaavy and exceeding Rystiggo.

What this means together: HCPs who are dissatisfied with Vyvgart are NOT defaulting to Rystiggo as the "next FcRn." Instead, they are choosing: (a) Imaavy for continuous FcRn coverage without cycle-off, or (b) complement inhibitors for a mechanism switch, or (c) Uplizna for the B-cell depletion approach. Rystiggo is being squeezed between Vyvgart (proven first-mover) and Imaavy (better continuous dosing profile). This is a critical strategic challenge for UCB.

Pattern 3: Zilbrysq's Daily Dosing Is Not Resonating — HCPs Aren't Even Offering It

Proposal conversation examples (Q4 2023 data): In the 21 earlier conversations ZoomRx used for the proposal, one PA described Zilbrysq as "a newer medication... injection once a day at home" with "really great results." The patient responded positively ("Okay, wow. Yeah."). This was a rare Zilbrysq success moment.

Phase 1 + Phase 2 (40 conversations): Zero Zilbrysq prescriptions. Only 4 total mentions, always as part of a list of options alongside 4-5 other brands. No conversation featured a HCP proactively recommending Zilbrysq as the preferred option.

Context from competitive landscape: Ultomiris (every 8 weeks IV) is the established C5 leader. Cemdisiran (every 12 weeks SC) is coming. Gefurulimab (monthly SC) is coming. Zilbrysq's daily injection is the most burdensome C5 dosing schedule on the market or in late-stage development.

What this means together: Zilbrysq's value proposition — "daily SC, self-administered at home, different mechanism from FcRn" — is theoretically compelling but practically not converting. HCPs either prefer the IV C5 options they know (Ultomiris) or are gravitating to FcRn inhibitors (Vyvgart, Imaavy) as the "modern" standard. Zilbrysq is in a no-man's-land: too burdensome for convenience-seeking patients, not differentiated enough for mechanism-seeking physicians.

Pattern 4: Patient Access and Cost Are Mentioned Repeatedly — UCB's ONWARD Program Is a Real Differentiator

Conv #08 (Rystiggo prescription): The HCP confirmed insurance clearance through the Onward program before recommending Rystiggo: "we got you on that onward program, which allows you to get the drug at a cheaper price... it's going to be close to $0 from what I hear." The patient agreed to proceed specifically because cost was addressed.

Conv #06 (Vyvgart outcome): HCPs position cost as a major consideration in gMG treatment decisions. Patient quotes include: "The other big thing I would like to see is lower cost." This suggests cost-sensitive patients could be a UCB target segment if access programs are differentiated.

Multiple conversations: Prior authorization and insurance approval discussions appear in most conversations involving advanced biologics. HCPs frequently mention cost as a barrier or express uncertainty about coverage.

What this means together: In gMG, the treatment decision is heavily gated by payer access. UCB's ONWARD program is a tangible differentiator that directly contributed to at least one Rystiggo prescription. If UCB can position ONWARD as providing faster, more reliable access than competitors' programs, this could shift the prescribing decision at the margin — especially for cost-sensitive or commercially insured patients.

Pattern 5: HCP Communication Style Is Predominantly Consultative — Patients Have Influence

Aug 14 analysis (N=14): 11 of 14 conversations (79%) were consultative — HCPs invited patient input, asked preferences, and offered choices. Only 3 were prescriptive (HCP directed unilaterally). Consultative examples: "Which one do you think you would kind of want to do?"; "Are you still comfortable?"; "Would you want to try...?"

What this means together: The consultative approach means patient preferences MATTER in gMG treatment decisions. Patients who research online, hear about a specific brand from support groups, or have strong convenience preferences can influence which biologic they receive. This validates patient-facing messaging (DTC, patient community programs) as a legitimate strategy in gMG — not just HCP promotion.

2. Thesis Check

Thesis 1: Vyvgart's first-mover moat is structural

Status: STRONGLY CONFIRMED 31 mentions, 17 prescriptions across 40 conversations. Rystiggo is positioned as "similar" but not "better." HCPs who move off Vyvgart go to Imaavy or Uplizna, not Rystiggo. UCB should focus on Vyvgart switchers and mechanism-differentiation, not head-to-head displacement.

Thesis 2: Zilbrysq's daily dosing is a terminal competitive liability

Status: CONFIRMED Zero prescriptions is a devastating data point. No HCP proactively recommended Zilbrysq. Pipeline C5 competitors (cemdisiran, gefurulimab) will only widen the gap. UCB has 12-18 months to prove Zilbrysq can acquire patients before the window closes.

Thesis 3: UCB's real opportunity is mechanism-choice partnerships

Status: DIRECTIONALLY SUPPORTED, INSUFFICIENT DATA HCPs do list multiple mechanisms when educating patients, and UCB's dual-brand position is structurally unique. But no conversation showed a HCP choosing UCB specifically because of the dual-brand proposition. Need more data (or a different research design) to test this.

Thesis 4: Uplizna's B-cell depletion model is the dark horse

Status: EARLY SIGNAL, WATCH CLOSELY 5 mentions and 3 prescriptions in Phase 2 alone — faster organic uptake than either UCB brand at comparable timepoints. "Two doses per year" is a powerful narrative. Too early to call paradigm shift but the signal is real.

Thesis 5: Vyvgart switching triggers are the most valuable finding

Status: CONFIRMED, NEEDS FINAL REPORT FRAMING Multiple conversations contain Vyvgart friction points (chest pain, cycle-off symptom recurrence, cost concerns). These are actionable for UCB positioning. ZoomRx should frame the final report around switching patterns, not just awareness/mention counts.

3. Data Gaps

  1. No final report yet — all findings are from check-in decks; coded analysis, thematic tagging, and strategic recommendations are pending (expected May 2026)
  2. Physician segmentation — limited ability to analyze by prescriber archetype (Vyvgart loyalist vs. mechanism explorer vs. access-driven) with current data granularity
  3. Patient outcomes — no longitudinal data on whether patients prescribed Rystiggo/Zilbrysq/Vyvgart remained on therapy or switched
  4. Regional variation — no data on geographic differences in prescribing patterns
  5. DTC impact — Zilbrysq DTC launch was planned during the study period; no data on whether DTC influenced patient awareness or requests
  6. MuSK+ segment — insufficient MuSK+ patients in the sample to draw conclusions about Rystiggo's unique positioning advantage

4. Recommendations for Final Report Framing

  1. Lead with switching triggers, not awareness metrics. UCB knows Vyvgart dominates. What they need is the specific clinical scenarios where physicians consider alternatives — and which alternative they choose in each scenario.

  2. Build a "mechanism-class decision tree" from the conversation data. When does the HCP choose FcRn vs. C5? What patient factors drive mechanism selection? This is more strategically valuable to UCB than individual brand mentions.

  3. Flag the Imaavy and Uplizna signals prominently. UCB needs to hear that their competitors are gaining traction faster than their own brands. The J&J and Amgen threats are real and accelerating.

  4. Position the ONWARD access program as a commercial lever. The data shows access/cost discussions in multiple conversations and at least one prescription driven by ONWARD. This is a concrete, actionable finding.

  5. Recommend a follow-up study on Zilbrysq-specific barriers. Zero prescriptions across 40 conversations demands a root-cause investigation. Is it daily dosing? Awareness? AChR-only limitation? HCP perception of complement class? A targeted qualitative study (8-10 IDIs) focused specifically on why HCPs don't prescribe Zilbrysq could be a high-value follow-on engagement.

This is the single most consistent finding across all available ZoomRx check-in data.
Proposal (March 2025): ZoomRx's own POV notes: "Among branded therapies, VYVGART is highly preferred by HCPs followed by ULTOMIRIS." Even before the study began, ZoomRx flagged Vyvgart's dominance.
